coredata.dc:description: Creating environmentally-friendly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (HCQDs) from human hair is a straightforward process with versatile applications. These HCQDs, derived from non-toxic hair, are produced through a one-step microwave (MW) method and have gained increasing attention due to their exceptional properties. Despite the availability of various advanced HCQDs, their full potential remains untapped. Thanks to their remarkable optical characteristics, HCQDs serve as highly sensitive nano-probe agents for the fluorometric measurement of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence (FL) intensity of the nano-probe exhibits a linear correlation with Fe3+ ion concentration ranging from 0 to 50 μM, boasting a limit of detection (LoD) of approximately 0.13 μM. This sensitivity is achieved through resonance energy/electron transfer-induced FL quenching. Remarkably, carbon dots (CDs) obtained from human hair function as ink-free patterned substrates, making them ideal for printing and the nondestructive collection and detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs). Immersing LFPs in HCQDs@poly(vinyl alcohol) (HCQDs@PVA) solution yields flexible, transparent films with consistent luminescent LFPs and clear ridge features for accurate personal identification. Importantly, this technique allows for the lifting and identification of long-term exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing any damage. The segregation of HCQDs within the polymer matrix during film formation is the mechanism responsible for LFP collection and visualization. Overall, HCQDs@PVA composites exhibit outstanding performance, suitable for applications in fluorescent patterns, flat panel displays, LFP analysis, and anti-counterfeiting (AC) labeling.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creating environmentally-friendly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (HCQDs) from human hair is a straightforward process with versatile applications. These HCQDs, derived from non-toxic hair, are produced through a one-step microwave (MW) method and have gained increasing attention due to their exceptional properties. Despite the availability of various advanced HCQDs, their full potential remains untapped. Thanks to their remarkable optical characteristics, HCQDs serve as highly sensitive nano-probe agents for the fluorometric measurement of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence (FL) intensity of the nano-probe exhibits a linear correlation with Fe3+ ion concentration ranging from 0 to 50 μM, boasting a limit of detection (LoD) of approximately 0.13 μM. This sensitivity is achieved through resonance energy/electron transfer-induced FL quenching. Remarkably, carbon dots (CDs) obtained from human hair function as ink-free patterned substrates, making them ideal for printing and the nondestructive collection and detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs). Immersing LFPs in HCQDs@poly(vinyl alcohol) (HCQDs@PVA) solution yields flexible, transparent films with consistent luminescent LFPs and clear ridge features for accurate personal identification. Importantly, this technique allows for the lifting and identification of long-term exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing any damage. The segregation of HCQDs within the polymer matrix during film formation is the mechanism responsible for LFP collection and visualization. Overall, HCQDs@PVA composites exhibit outstanding performance, suitable for applications in fluorescent patterns, flat panel displays, LFP analysis, and anti-counterfeiting (AC) labeling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based materials have gained significant research interest and found widespread use in various fields. They offer appealing features like water solubility, low toxicity, easy functionalization, resistance to photo-bleaching, chemical stability, and bio-compatibility [1–3]. These materials serve as attractive alternatives to hazardous metal-based quantum dots (QDs) and find applications in drug delivery, bio-imaging, and bio-sensing [4,5]. Among the various carbon-based materials explored by researchers, CDs have garnered significant attention. They stand out for their strong FL, customizable properties, and simple synthesis methods using readily available and cost-effective raw materials [6–8]. CDs offer a promising avenue for converting waste materials into advanced nano-materials, promoting sustainability. They can be synthesized from various waste sources like orange peels, fish scales, scrap paper, onion remnants, and even human hair [9–13]. Human hair is particularly interesting due to its accessibility and keratin-rich protein content. It introduces nitrogen elements into CDs, altering their photo-luminescence properties, such as quantum yield (QY), and enhancing surface functionalities [14,15]. Efforts to improve QY have been made. Liu et al. achieved a notable peak photo-luminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 17 % using pyrolysis [16]. Guo et al. developed solvent-free CDs from hair with a synthesis yield of 95 %, albeit with a slightly lower QY of 10.75 % [17]. Hou et al. used the hydrothermal method, obtaining CDs from hair in water with an exceptional QY of 24.8 %. Importantly, all these studies observed that CDs derived from hair were sensitive to Fe3+ ions in water, as shown by their photo-luminescence (PL) response [18].

This study presents a novel one-step MW synthesis method that quickly produces highly luminescent CDs from non-toxic raw materials, particularly human hair waste, in just 5 min. This approach eliminates the need for hazardous solvents or expensive starting materials and yields CDs with exceptional blue FL, negating the necessity for additional surface treatments. These CDs disperse effectively in polymer matrices like PMMA and PVP [19,20]. Leveraging this compatibility, we have successfully created transparent fluorescent films suitable for AC and flexible display applications. See Table 1 [21–40] for a literature review summarizing various aspects of CDs and their applications, providing context for the present work. Expanding on our discoveries, we present a novel application for CDs/polymer composites as ink-free patterned substrate films. These films are exceptionally useful for detecting and extracting LFPs, with potential applications in the printing industry. Fingerprint recognition plays a vital role in various domains, including forensics, access control, and personal identification. However, LFPs are often invisible to the naked eye, necessitating advanced techniques for visualization. While CDs have been explored for FP recovery and ink [41], concerns remain regarding rapid degradation and FL loss. Our study pioneers the use of CDs composites as ink-free patterned substrates in the printing industry [42], offering promising applications in AC and efficient LFP identification.

Further, we create innovative ink-free substrates for LFP identification and printing using CDs derived from biomass and incorporated into a polymer matrix. These CDs are produced through a one-step heating process using non-toxic hair fiber material, offering a cost-effective and eco-friendly source of green carbon. The synthesis is straightforward, economical, and environmentally benign. These CDs possess remarkable properties, including strong amphiphilicity and excellent solubility in various solvents, crucial for future applications. By blending these fluorescent CDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), we develop transparent composite films that serve as pattern substrates without the need for conventional ink. These films allow for the non-destructive capture of LFPs on diverse surfaces, enabling their examination under UV light. This approach simplifies personal identification using readily available, non-toxic materials with stable, customizable colors. Furthermore, these films enable repeated LFP retrieval without causing damage and can visualize relief patterns and designs from various surfaces, eliminating the need for traditional ink. These composite materials can also produce fluorescent ink suitable for handwriting or screen printing.

We provide a versatile platform for various applications utilizing CDs, such as anti-counterfeiting measures and latent fingerprint identification, offering innovative solutions in this field. Monitoring toxic heavy metal ions, particularly Fe3+, in the environment is crucial due to their harmful effects on ecosystems and human health. Iron, a key transition metal ion, plays essential roles in biological systems. Imbalances in Fe3+ levels can lead to health issues, from cancer to anaemia, emphasizing the importance of accurate detection methods. Given the paramount importance of detecting Fe3+ ions in both the environment and human health, the development of a sensitive, selective, and safe detection method assumes great significance. FL spectroscopy has gained popularity as a detection method due to its high sensitivity, ease of use, and remarkable reproducibility. In this study, we introduce a solvent-free, environmentally friendly synthesis method for the preparation of CDs through microwave synthesis. These CDs are nitrogen-doped and derived from human hair obtained from a single, common source under carefully controlled conditions. This research aims to contribute to the development of a robust and efficient method for detecting Fe3+ ions, addressing a critical need in environmental monitoring and human health assessment.

In this section, we describe the materials and methods employed in our study. Human hairs were collected using individual hair combs for the experiment. We sourced aluminium oxide powder and octadecane (average particle size <1 μm) from Sigma Aldrich. It is worth noting that no additional purification of these reagents was necessary, as they were all of analytical quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of HCQDs via microwave oven
ce:section.ce:para: To produce human hair carbon quantum dots (HCQDs) using a microwave oven, we initiated the process by washing 10 g of human hair fibers in a substantial amount of distilled water while subjecting them to sonication for a duration of 60 min. Subsequently, we performed additional washes using copious amounts of acetone and distilled water, again utilizing sonication. Following this, the hair fiber sample was dried within a hot air oven, maintaining a temperature of 60 °C. The modified sample was then placed into a ceramic crucible and subjected to microwave irradiation at 800 W for a total duration of 8 min. This innovative technique involves heating the sample while it is exposed to the surrounding environment. After the microwave treatment, the resulting material was combined with 100 ml of water and subjected to sonication for a duration of 1 h, ensuring a homogeneous dispersion of HCQDs in the water. To eliminate any undesirable reaction by-products, a filtration process was employed, utilizing a column containing aluminium oxide particles. Fig. 1a provides a conceptual representation of the HCQDs synthesis process for visualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of anti-counterfeiting ink using HCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To craft the AC ink, we combine 5 g of PVA with HCQDs. Achieving a homogeneous dispersion is crucial, 0.5 g of HCQDs undergoes sonication for a duration of 60 min within the PVA medium. The solution is then degassed in a vacuum pumping system for 10 min to eliminate air bubbles. Once this transparent solution is obtained, it is immediately put to use as security ink. Fig. 1b offers a schematic representation of the screen-printing method and the sketch pen mode employed while utilizing the anti-counterfeit ink. The AC labels, when printed or written with this luminescent ink, can be illuminated using UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, and images can subsequently be captured using a camera.

In the process of creating HCQDs/PVA composites, we began by adding 15 g PVA solid to 100 ml of deionized (DI) water in a three-necked, round-bottomed flask equipped with a stirrer. This solution was stirred for 60 min while being heated to 100 °C. Subsequently, the aforementioned solution was combined with aqueous solution of HCQDs. After 30 min of stirring, an aqueous solution of HCQDs@PVA composites was successfully generated. The HCQDs@PVA mixture was carefully deposited into a Petri plate and maintained at 45 °C for a duration of 60 min (Fig. 1c). The resulting flexible film layer, comprising HCQDs, was peeled off and reserved for various applications.

Thanks to their remarkable solubility and fluorescence, HCQDs are effectively dispersed within the polymer matrix. This dispersion quality leads to the creation of HCQDs@PVA composites that prove invaluable for the identification of LFPs. For a comprehensive understanding of the LFPs data collection and visualization process (Fig. 1d and e). Additionally, Fig. 1f provides a visual representation of the practical technique employed to non-destructively collect LFPs on a glass slide using HCQDs@PVA composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HCQDs@PVA composites as ink-free patterned substrates
ce:section.ce:para: To create a flexible transparent film, we apply an aqueous solution of HCQDs@PVA composites onto a surface containing LFPs. After allowing the solvents to evaporate, the film is carefully peeled away from the surface, revealing a stable and fluorescent fingerprint pattern.

Our sensing analysis involves testing for the presence of various metal ions in drinking water, including Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cd2+, and Ca2+ at a concentration of 15 μM in 1 ml of water. We then add 2 μL of the HCQDs solution drop by drop to the metal stock solution and agitate the mixture with a magnetic stirrer for 15 min. Remarkably, the solution containing iron metal ions undergoes a noticeable colour change from blue to black. Given the sensitivity of the HCQDs solution to iron metal ions, we proceed to study its FL emission and other properties.

The following equation is employed to estimate the QY, with quinine sulfate (QS) as reference (0.1 M H2SO4 with a QY, ɸstd = 54 % at 360 nm) [43].

where QY is the CQD's FL quantum yield and φstd is the standard QS fluorescence QY. Isample and Istd represents the integrated FL intensity of HCQDs and QS, respectively. Asample and Astd describes the estimated values of HCQDs and standard QS, ηsample and ηstd depicts the refractive indices of the HCQDs and standard QS. To avoid the re-absorption effects, the intensity of absorbance should be less than 0.1 for all samples.

We employ a range of techniques to thoroughly characterize the human hair-derived CQDs. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM: Hitachi H-8100) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are utilized to image the CDs, with the average size and size dispersion calculated from approximately 1000 CDs. We record the absorption spectra of each sample using the Cary 60 G6860A spectrophotometer (Agilent). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is conducted using a PerkinElmer spectrometer to investigate molecular bonds. Vibrational spectra of the HCQDs molecular bonds are obtained through Raman spectroscopy using a Via (Renishaw) Raman spectrometer with a 785 nm laser source. For X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, we use the Axis Supra (Kratos) instrument with aluminium Al Kα (hυ = 1486.7 eV) as the X-ray source. The Horiba Spectrofluorometer (Fluorolog-3) is employed to record the photoluminescence spectra of both excitation and emission for the HCQDs. Additionally, we conduct photoluminescence (PL) spectra experiments for sensing applications using the Ocean Optic Spectrometer.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of HCQDs reveal a single broad peak, centred at 2θ = 21.84°, corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of graphitic carbon, indicating a graphite-like structure (Fig. 2 a). The larger inter-layer spacing (d = 0.37 nm) compared to graphite (0.34 nm) suggests the presence of oxygen-containing or organic functional groups on the surface, indicating incomplete graphitization [44]. Raman analysis further confirms the graphitic nature of HCQDs, showing two broad peaks at 1352 and 1546 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively (Fig. 2b). The D band signifies disorder in the graphite lattice and is related to the presence of sp3 defects, while the G band corresponds to sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a hexagonal lattice [45]. The integrated intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) for HCQDs is approximately 0.66, indicating a somewhat disordered graphite-like structure and providing insight into the degree of disorder and functionalization (sp3/sp2 carbon ratio) [46].

Various functional groups have been introduced to the surface of CDs, and their characteristics are examined through FT-IR spectrum analysis. As shown in Fig. 2c, peaks around 3473 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups. Additionally, the presence of carboxyl groups is indicated by peaks at 1691 cm−1 and 1489 cm−1, while C–O/C–N stretching vibrations are observed in the range of 1040–1500 cm−1 [47,48]. Furthermore, X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) is employed to determine the elemental composition and valence state of the synthesized HCQDs. The full-scan survey spectrum in Fig. 2d reveals the absence of additional impurities and confirms the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) elements in the CDs. The distinctive peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s are detected at binding energies of 285 eV, 398.5 eV, and 532 eV, respectively, with atomic ratios of 65 %, 5 %, and 30 %, respectively. These findings provide additional evidence for the presence of enhanced N- and O-containing functional groups within or on the surface of the carbon structure, enhancing its hydrophilicity.

To gain deeper insights into the chemical bonding information, the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s binding energy levels are meticulously deconvoluted and fitted using the Casa XPS tool. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum, as depicted in Fig. 2e, exhibits five distinct peaks corresponding to different carbon bonding groups: CC/C–C, C–N–C, C–O–C/C–OH, CO/CN, and OC–OH/OC–NH at binding energies of 284.4, 285.5, 286.2, 287.0, and 288.2 eV, respectively [49,50]. In addition to these peaks, a satellite peak is discerned as a broad and small feature at 290.7 eV in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum. The successful incorporation of nitrogen (N) atoms into or onto the carbon structure of HCQDs is evident in the high-resolution N 1s spectra, which can be deconvoluted into distinct peaks representing pyridinic C–N–C (398.2 eV), pyrrolic N–H (399.6 eV), and graphitic (C)3–N bonds (400.1 eV) (Fig. 2f). Furthermore, the high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. 2g) reveal peaks corresponding to CO (531.3 eV), C–OH/C–O–C (532.7 eV), OC–OH bonds (533.1 eV), and H–O–H groups within or on the carbon framework of HCQDs, represented by tiny peaks at 535.6 eV [51,52]. These observations provide compelling evidence for the presence of N and O elements within the synthesized HCQDs and the existence of N and O-containing functional groups on their surface, including OH, COOH, NH, and CN. These functional groups act as chromophores or auxo-chromes, enhancing FL emission and, consequently, the fluorescent activity. Additionally, they contribute to the hydrophilicity of HCQDs, facilitating their dispersion in aqueous solutions, which is advantageous for various applications such as metal ion detection, fluorescent ink, fluorescent bio-imaging, and more [53,54].

To investigate the morphology and particle size of HCQDs, TEM was employed (Fig. 2h). The TEM images reveal that the synthesized HCQDs possess a spherical shape with diameters measuring approximately 3–5 nm. Remarkably, these individual HCQDs exhibit uniform dispersion without any signs of aggregation. The lattice plane corresponding to (100) exhibits an inter-planar spacing of 0.22 nm. Notably, alongside the mono-dispersed HCQDs, aggregated and distinctly defined highly crystalline HCQDs are also observed (Fig. 2i). In this case, the lattice planes of (100) correspond to measured inter-planar spacing's of 0.222 nm. Upon analyzing the particle size histogram (Fig. 2j), it was determined that the majority of particles fell within a size range of 3–5 nm, with the most prevalent size range spanning from 2 to 8 nm.

The optical properties of the synthesized HCQDs were thoroughly examined through UV–Vis and FL spectroscopy, with the results depicted in Fig. 3 . In the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 3a), two distinct absorption peaks are clearly evident at 273 nm and 322 nm. These peaks correspond to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the conjugated CO/CN bonds, respectively. Notably, the emission spectrum slightly surpasses the excitation spectrum, revealing two peaks. The proximity of the additional FL excitation peak to the 322 nm absorption peak is a clear indication that this new peak is a result of the n-π* transition emission [55,56]. Fig. 3b illustrates the FL excitation and emission spectra. The optimal excitation wavelength is determined to be 450 nm, with a maximum emission wavelength of 362 nm. The proximity of the FL emission peak at 450 nm to the absorption band at 322 nm confirms that the emission is triggered by the n-p* transition associated with conjugated CO and CN bonds. Furthermore, as the excitation wavelength is varied from 300 to 410 nm, the maximum emission wavelengths exhibit gradual increases, ranging from 430 to 500 nm (Fig. 3c).

The HCQDs exhibit strong excitation-dependent emission characteristics, suggesting their suitability for various FL detection applications. As depicted in Fig. 3d, the FL intensity of HCQDs was measured under different durations of UV light (365 nm with power intensity 6 mW/cm2) exposure to assess their FL stability or photostability. Remarkably, there were no discernible changes in HCQDs FL intensity during irradiation periods ranging from 0 to 60 min. Even after continuous exposure for 60 min, there was no sign of photobleaching, indicating the robustness of HCQDs structure and their resistance to disruption by UV conditions. To visually demonstrate the FL stability of HCQDs, digital images of aqueous HCQDs were captured at various exposure times under a 365 nm UV lamp, as shown in the inset of Fig. 4 d (visible to the naked eye). Both the 0-min and 60-min irradiation images of aqueous HCQDs displayed a vibrant blue colour, easily visible to the naked eye under a 365 nm UV lamp. This observation underscores the long-term FL stability of HCQDs. In addition, by utilizing UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectrum of the quinine sulfate and HCQD solutions are utilized to estimate the QY (Fig. 3e and f). The estimated QY of the HCQDs is found to be 16 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical sensing performance of the HCQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The unique properties of the synthesized HCQDs make them an excellent choice as a luminescent based nano sensor for the direct detection of metal ions in aqueous solutions using a fluorometric technique. In Fig. 4a, we explored changes in FL intensity when introducing metal ions, including Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+,Cr3+, Co2+ and Cd2+ each at a concentration of 1 mM, to evaluate the selectivity of this sensing system. Notably, among all the tested metal ions, the addition of Fe3+ ions induced the most significant decrease in FL intensity in the HCQDs solution. This reduction in FL intensity arises from the interactions between Fe3+ ions and HCQDs, suggesting a strong concentration of FL within the presence of Fe3+ ions. When various metal ions are introduced, the FL quenching effect becomes apparent, leading to a decline in emission intensity [57]. By calculating the quenching constants for different metal ions using the Stern-Volmer equation (Eqn. (2)) [58] (Fig. 4b), it becomes evident that the addition of Fe3+ ions to the HCQD solution results in the most pronounced quenching effect.
(2)

In this context, where F0: FL intensity of the HCQD solution, F stands for the FL intensity of the metal ion solution, and KSν represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. Furthermore, we observed the effects of Fe3+ ion-induced FL quenching in the HCQD solution as various concentrations (0–50 μM) of Fe3+ ions were introduced. As shown in Fig. 4c, with increasing Fe3+ ion concentration from 0 to 50 μM, the FL intensity of HCQDs gradually decreases while the emission wavelength remains unchanged. The corresponding graph can be seen in the inset of Fig. 4c, depicting the FL intensity variations concerning Fe3+ concentration. These spectral observations indicate a strong linear correlation and a linear quenching process (R2 = 0.990). Using equation (3), the calculated LoD is 0.13 μM. Therefore, these results affirm that the presence of Fe3+ ions efficiently and selectively quenches the FL of the synthesized HCQDs. Additionally, Fig. 4d presents visual evidence of FL HCQDs in the presence of various metal ions, including Al3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Co2+ and Cd2+ each at a concentration of 1000 mM, under both daylight and UV 365 nm light. The FL quenching observed upon the addition of Fe3+ ions suggests the formation of an HCQDs + Fe3+ adduct, potentially due to active functional groups interacting with Fe3+ ions, as indicated by naked eye observations. This finding is in line with existing literature data, as presented in Table 2 [59–86], providing a comparison of CQDs obtained from various sources for Fe3+ sensing. Notably, the LoD of 0.13 μM achieved in this study is significantly lower than previously reported LoDs for Fe3+ ion sensing.
(3)

=
3 σ
S

In the equation, S represents the slope of the linear calibration plot, and σ represents the standard deviation of the signals (n = 6). The formation of HCQDs as a result of electrostatic interaction and coordination between Al3+ ions and HCQDs can be attributed to several factors. Al3+ ions form aggregates that generate conjugated amorphous masses, resulting in enhanced FL emission due to aggregation effects (Fig. 4e). The significant finding here is that HCQDs, as synthesized, exhibit dual FL response characteristics with remarkable selectivity for both Al3+ and Fe3+ ions.

Fig. 5 a illustrates the quenching and re-quenching behaviour of HCQDs. The addition of 15 μM ascorbic acid (AA) to the mixture successfully re-quenches the FL of HCQDs. Interestingly, the colour of the HCQDs solution changes from black to blue, indicating the recovery of FL properties. This restoration of FL is believed to occur due to the release of loosely bound Fe3+ ions from the surface of HCQDs, given the strong affinity between AA and Fe3+.The quenching and re-quenching mechanism of FL is employed for turn ON-OFF experiments, and the performance of the logic gate (LG) is also investigated. Fig. 6 a provides a schematic representation of the FL ON-OFF assay involving CDs obtained from various sources.

Logic gates (LGs) are fundamental components of logical circuits, capable of receiving input signals and generating output values based on defined operations. In this study, we harness the ability of the HCQDs solution to quench FL upon the addition of Fe3+ ions and to re-quench it with AA. Specifically, we explore the behaviour of NOT and IMPLICATION LGs to assess the quenching and re-quenching properties of HCQDs under various input scenarios [87].The NOT gate, often referred to as an inverter, represents a fundamental logic gate equipped with a single input signal. Its primary function is logical negation, effectively reversing the input and output states. For each NOT gate, there exists a solitary input signal. When the input is set to 1, the output becomes 0, and conversely, when the input is 0, the output is 1. This unique behavior forms the basis for our experiment, where we create and assess the operation of a NOT gate. We apply this concept to the quenching behavior of HCQDs: input 1 corresponds to the presence of Fe3+, while input 0 signifies the absence of Fe3+. Remarkably, the HCQDs solution exhibits its maximum FL intensity when Fe3+ is absent, resulting in an output of 1 (ON state). Conversely, in the presence of Fe3+, FL is quenched, leading to an output of 0 (OFF state). The IMPLICATION LG establishes logical connections between pairs of propositions and is characterized by two inputs. One input interfaces with an OR gate, while the other input passes through a NOT gate before feeding into the second input of the OR gate. Consequently, the output of the IMPLICATION gate corresponds to the output of the OR gate.

In our experiment, we employ Fe3+ and AA as input states “1” and “0” respectively. By observing the behaviour of the HCQDs solution under these input conditions, we establish a comprehensive truth table and illustrate the operation of the IMPLICATION gate. Fig. 5b provides a detailed depiction of the NOT logic gate, complemented by its corresponding truth table. The operation of the NOT gate revolves around the FL quenching phenomenon exhibited by HCQDs. In this context, input 1 signifies the presence of Fe3+, while input 0 denotes the absence of Fe3+. Interestingly, the HCQDs solution exhibits its highest FL intensity in the absence of Fe3+, resulting in an output state of 1 (ON state). Conversely, when Fe3+ is present, the HCQDs solution ceases to fluoresce, leading to an output state of 0 (OFF state). These observations form the basis for constructing the truth table and illustrating the logical functioning of the NOT gate.Moving forward, we delve into the realm of the LG IMPLY logic gate, which establishes a logical connection between two propositions. This gate comprises two inputs, one of which is connected to an input of an OR gate. The second input, however, passes through a NOT gate, which inverts its output before feeding it into the second input of the OR gate. Consequently, the output of the IMPLY gate is assumed to align with the output of the OR gate. This foundational understanding serves as the basis for constructing and evaluating the performance of the IMPLICATION gate, and examine its performance.Exploring these logical gates within the context of HCQDs FL properties paves the way for exciting possibilities in the development of advanced logic circuits and molecular-level computational devices.

Fig. 5c offers an intricate glimpse into the IMPLICATION logic gate, accompanied by its corresponding truth table. The IMPLICATION gate is constructed upon the foundation of Fe3+ and AA-sensitive switching technology, where Fe3+ and AA signify the input state “1,” while their absence represents the “0″ state. This configuration results in four distinct input conditions, each with its associated output. In the absence of both Fe3+ and AA (0/0 state), the HCQDs solution exhibits its most intense FL, yielding an output of 1 (ON state). Conversely, when Fe3+ is present (0/1) and AA is absent, the HCQDs solution ceases to fluoresce, resulting in an output of 0 (OFF state). When AA is present (1/0) without Fe3+, the HCQDs solution regains its FL, generating an output of 1 (ON state). Finally, when both AA and Fe3+ coexist, FL is observed, and the output once again registers as 1 (ON state). These outcomes underscore the remarkable potential of synthesized HCQDs in advancing the realm of molecular logic devices, offering promising prospects for future developments in this exciting field.

The HCQDs are demonstrated as a promising illumination material for anti-counterfeiting (AC) applications. This versatile material allows for secure and convenient encryption and decryption of information when exposed to 365 nm UV light, offering an effective means of safeguarding sensitive data [88]. To assess its practicality, we employed screen-printing technology to create fluorescent patterns on various surfaces, including standard fluorescent paper, cellulose paper, cardboard sheets, and aluminum foil. In broad daylight (Fig. 6a), these patterns remained invisible, leaving no discernible impressions. However, when illuminated with 365 nm UV light, vibrant blue patterns emerged, underscoring the effectiveness of HCQDs-based FL ink for security applications. To further evaluate the stability of the AC patterns created with HCQDs FL ink, we conducted a 90-day storage test. Even after this extended period (Fig. 6b), the emission color of the AC pattern remained unchanged. This robust and consistent blue emission highlights the environmental resilience of HCQDs-based FL ink, affirming its suitability for secure applications. These results provide compelling evidence of the effectiveness and durability of the HCQDs FL ink in security applications [89].

The stability and photo-stability of HCQDs are paramount for practical applications. In Fig. 6c, we present the FL emission spectra of HCQDs solutions at varying UV exposure times (10–120 min). Remarkably, the FL intensities of HCQDs remain nearly unchanged from the initial synthesis to day 90 of preservation. These minimal intensity fluctuations underscore the long-term persistence of HCQDs. Fig. 6c illustrates the exceptional photo-stability of AC patterns before and after exposure to 365 nm UV radiation for 120 min. The FL intensity of the irradiated AC pattern exhibits negligible differences compared to the non-irradiated pattern, underscoring the remarkable photo-stability of HCQDs. To assess the stability of AC patterns under varying temperatures, we subjected patterns drawn on marble to temperatures ranging from 100 to 160 °C for 30 min (Fig. 6d). Impressively, the patterns remain distinct and consistent up to 140 °C. These findings highlight HCQDs as a potential replacement for conventional FL inks due to their robust FL, high quantum yield, solubility, excellent FL stability, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness [90]. The synthesized HCQDs aqueous solution can be readily utilized in sketch pens without any modifications, allowing for writing or drawing on paper with ease.

Fig. 7 a and b vividly illustrate the intense blue FL emitted by handwritten text and hand-drawn images, both clearly discernible under UV light at 365 nm. To assess FL stability, the hand-drawn image is compared with its stored counterpart over an extended period. Remarkably, the FL intensity of the hand-drawn image remains largely unchanged even after 120 days of storage (Fig. 7c), attesting to the excellent FL stability of HCQDs.Furthermore, the resulting FL ink is transparent, environmentally friendly, durable, biocompatible, and easily cleanable [91,92]. This makes HCQDs aqueous solution an attractive option for fluorescent sketch pens. When viewed in daylight, handwritten text and hand-drawn images are colorless or nearly invisible, highlighting HCQDs potential for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.Continuing our exploration of practical features, the stability and photo-stability of HCQDs are thoroughly examined. In Fig. 8 d, the FL emission spectra of HCQDs solutions are presented at varying UV exposure times (10–120 min). The FL intensities of HCQDs remain almost consistent from the initial synthesis to day 90 of preservation, indicating their long-term stability. Fig. 7d demonstrates the exceptional photo-stability of AC patterns before and after 120 min of exposure to 365 nm UV radiation from a UV lamp. Impressively, the FL intensity of the irradiated AC pattern shows little variation compared to the non-irradiated pattern, underscoring HCQDs remarkable photo-stability. To assess the stability of AC patterns under temperature variations, we examine patterns drawn on marble when subjected to temperatures ranging from 100 to 140 °C for 30 min (Fig. 7e). The results clearly show that the patterns remain distinct and reliable even at temperatures up to 140 °C.

The transparent security ink, when exposed to UV light at 365 nm, emits a brilliant blue radiance (Fig. 8a). To illustrate the creation of a UV-triggered hidden pattern film using this security ink, Fig. 8b and c provide a schematic representation. This process offers a straightforward means of implementing an AC code based on a predefined pattern. The ink is subsequently cast and dried, as depicted in Fig. 8d, under 365 nm UV light to yield the transparent AC film, which showcases remarkable flexibility and fold ability. In Fig. 8e and f, the resulting AC film is revealed to be optically transparent, boasting an overall transmittance of 88 % and an optical haze of just 6 % at 550 nm. Given the rapid pace of technological advancement, counterfeit information and products have become increasingly prevalent. To address this issue and promote AC packaging featuring the aforementioned advantages, HCQDs are dispersed into a PVA medium to craft a transparent AC film. Fig. 8g offers photographic views of the HCQDs@PVA films undergoing various folds, demonstrating their suitability for numerous applications within the realm of flexible electronics. This visual evidence confirms that when subjected to UV 365 nm excitation light, they emit a vivid blue glow, underscoring that PVA does not hinder the excellent performance of HCQDs in photo-luminescence assessments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and recognition of latent fingerprints
ce:section.ce:para: HCQDs are effectively dispersed within the polymer matrix, exhibiting excellent solubility and FL properties, rendering HCQDs/polymer composites highly suitable for detecting latent LFPs. We conducted an experiment where we applied HCQDs@PVA aqueous solution droplets onto surfaces, including LFPs-deposited paper, an overhead projector (OHP) sheet, and a glass slide [93–95]. As the solvent evaporated, it left behind a transparent, peelable film imprinted with the characteristic ridge and furrow patterns of LFPs. Since the lipid residues in FPs are insoluble in water, this process does not damage the imprinted LFPs pattern on the substrate, allowing for their preservation and retrieval using the HCQDs@PVA film. Under UV light, these films reveal well-defined FPs with distinct ridge characteristics. Previous reports have utilized HCQDs derived from p-phenylenediamine, citric acid, or citrate as fluorescent dusting agents or solutions to enhance LFPs. However, issues such as CDs FL quenching and corrosive FP patterns have persisted. Furthermore, CDs materials have not demonstrated the non-destructive capture of LFPs. In contrast, our approach involves quickly and cost-effectively developing LFPs on a portable, flexible, transparent film that exhibits vivid fluorescent ridge details. This method is invaluable for the identification and analysis of LFPs, especially when dealing with challenging background interference. The HCQDs dispersed in PVA are employed to lift LFPs from various surfaces. Additionally, the luminous FPs formed on the composite films exhibit exceptional durability, lasting for up to 365 days of exposure to the atmosphere.

We explored the potential of CQDs@polymer composites for practical LFPs detection across various surfaces. Detecting LFPs on diverse surfaces presents a significant challenge. We assessed the utility of CQDs@PVA composites for identifying LFPs on various surfaces using the method outlined in the experimental section. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, under UV 365 nm light, the FPs on HCQDs@PVA films transferred from paper, OHP sheets, and glass are all highly resolved and clearly visible. The 3D interactive plots of the generated FPs on all surfaces (Fig. 9b) reveal that the stained NPs are uniformly distributed across the surface of the FPs. This uniform distribution is evident in the 3D interactive plots of the generated FPs on all surfaces. Due to their nano-scale size and superior adhesive properties, the HCQDs adhere significantly to the ridges rather than the furrows, as demonstrated by the grayscale pixel profiles within the designated yellow boxes on the developed FPs on all surfaces (Fig. 9c). The green values are notably higher in the ridge regions but much lower in the furrow regions.

In an illustrative example, we employed HCQDs@PVA film to recover LFPs from a glass slide that had been exposed to the air for 1 day (Fig. 9d) and again after 365 days (Fig. 9e). In both cases, crystal-clear, unfading FPs were observed on the HCQDs@PVA films. These findings suggest that this technique could be employed to recover LFPs left behind in the past, and the ability to repeatedly recover FPs demonstrates that it does not damage the original LFPs. Furthermore, we found that no amount of external force could remove the FPs created by the HCQDs@PVA film. To visualize the retrieved features under 365 nm excitation on a glass slide using HCQDs, we examined both day 1 and day 365 aged FPs. Both clearly displayed level 1–2 traits. However, in the day 1 aged FP, level 3 features were also detectable with minimal FL quenching compared to the 365-day-old FP, underscoring the suitability of HCQDs for LFPs visualization applications [96–98]. Additionally, the pixel profiles for the produced FPs on glass slides for both day 1 and day 365 aged FPs were plotted (Fig. 9d1 & e1). These profiles reveal substantial differences in the ridges and furrows of the 365-day-old FP. In contrast, these changes were barely noticeable in the case of the day 1 aged FP.

In recap, we have successfully synthesized blue-emitting CQDs from human hair using the Microwave-assisted reflux method. These HCQDs exhibit a spherical morphology with an average diameter of approximately 3–5 nm. Additionally, the surface of HCQDs is adorned with functional groups, including oxygen, which enhances their water solubility and FL. The obtained HCQDs boast a respectable FL quantum yield (QY) of 16 %. Leveraging their FL properties, we have employed HCQDs as a sensitive probe for detecting Fe3+ ions, which effectively and selectively quench the HCQDs FL. This quenching response demonstrated an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.9932) with a LoD as low as 0.13 μM within a concentration range of 0–50 μM. Furthermore, we have constructed and evaluated NOT and IMPLICATION logic gates based on the fluorescent turn ON-OFF behavior of HCQDs when exposed to AA. Additionally, we have developed a fluorescent ink suitable for writing letters and creating patterns, facilitating encryption and decryption operations. In essence, these HCQDs, synthesized in 5 min, exhibit favorable characteristics for Fe3+ ion detection. To extend their applications, we blended HCQDs, derived from an abundant and non-toxic source, with PVA to create flexible and transparent films. This method shows promise for lifting and identifying long-term exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing any damage. Moreover, the materials used are readily available and benign. This study underscores the success of inter facial segregation of HCQDs in polymer films, opening up possibilities for a wide range of applications, including LFPs identification, robust ink-free printing, Fe3+ sensing, and flexible display technologies.

D. H. S, B. R. R: Investigation and writing original draft, S.C. S, H. N: Conceptualization and Supervision, K. C., J. I., D. F., Fr. A. G., N. R. N: Resources, Formal analysis, K. L: Sample synthesis.
